title: 4.3 and 4.4 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 4.3 and 4.4!
topic_path: Empires and Belief Systems (200 to 1500 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the concept of *syncretism*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The blending of different religious or cultural traditions into a unique belief system.
The strict rejection of foreign beliefs to preserve traditional practices.
The complete replacement of one religion by another after conquest.
The isolation of cultures to prevent outside influence or change.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Silk Road help promote religious syncretism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It connected diverse peoples who shared and blended religious beliefs and practices.
It forced local populations to abandon their religions and adopt new ones.
It isolated regions, helping them preserve their original religious beliefs.
It allowed trade but prevented the spread of religious teachings and traditions.

assessment_items.question_content:
The image below is of the Dunhuang cave murals along the Silk Road. This mural shows Buddhist figures with Chinese artistic styles and local elements.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What does this image suggest about the spread of Buddhism along the Silk Road?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The Buddha is centered in the image and posed in traditional meditative and teaching mudras. His robes and facial features follow Chinese stylistic norms. He is accompanied by guardians and soldiers who resemble Chinese guardian deities and officials.
options.caption: *The Mongao Caves*, [Creative Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mogao_Caves_(54376977152).jpg)

choices.content: Buddhism blended with local Chinese traditions, creating a unique regional form.
Buddhism remained unchanged even as it entered different regions like China.
Chinese people replaced their traditional beliefs with foreign Buddhist practices.
Foreign religions like Buddhism were banned from incorporating Chinese symbols.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the political structure of the Persian Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A highly centralized monarchy with local governors (satraps) who reported to the king
A direct democracy in which citizens vote on all major decisions
A loose alliance of independent city-states with no central authority
A theocratic system where religious leaders held ultimate political power

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the political structures of the Greek city-states leave a lasting legacy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They introduced citizen participation and inspired later democratic systems.
They permanently replaced monarchies across Europe with city-state governments.
They created the first global empire through centralized imperial rule.
They rejected all written laws and relied solely on oral tradition to govern.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the Macedonian Empire collapse so quickly after the death of its emperor, Alexander the Great (also known as Alexander of Macedon)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The empire’s strength depended heavily on Alexander’s extraordinary personality, but his successors lacked his vision and drive.
Despite his many military successes, Alexander was widely disliked by his subjects, so after his death, much of his empire unraveled.
Alexander’s attempts to spread Hellenistic (Greek) culture met resistance, and many Greeks saw his death as a chance to reclaim power.
Naming the city of Alexandria after himself made Alexander appear arrogant, causing his subjects to reject his accomplishments after his death.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Alexander’s conquest of Persia affect trade in the region?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Trade expanded dramatically, aided significantly by Alexander introducing gold and silver coins into the economy.
The empire grew so large that most resources were transported internally, causing a decline in trade with regions outside the empire.
Cultural differences between Persians and Greeks made trade nearly impossible.
Trade got a quick boost at first because new things like spices and metals became available, but it dropped off later.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How was society organized in the Ptolemaic Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Greek elites held most of the power, while native Egyptians were placed in lower social and political roles.
All social groups had equal access to political power, regardless of ethnicity or background.
Egyptian priests ruled alongside Greek generals in a shared power structure.
Native Egyptians held the authority in government, while the minority Greek settlers had limited power.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Greek rule during the Ptolemaic Dynasty affect religious practices in Egypt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Greek rulers supported Egyptian religion and introduced Serapis, a god blending Greek and Egyptian beliefs.
The Greeks tried to replace Egyptian gods with Greek ones and forced Egyptians to change how they worshipped.
Egyptian religion stayed the same because the Greeks weren’t religious and only cared about political control.
Social status depended on worship, so Egyptians converted to Greek religion to move up in society.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which feature of the Mauryan Empire's government was inspired by Persian rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The use of regional governors to help manage distant provinces
The creation of a council of philosophers to make all major political decisions
A government based entirely on direct democracy and citizen voting
The rejection of written laws in favor of oral tradition and tribal councils

assessment_items.question_content:
Although Ashoka was known as a skilled military leader, he expanded and strengthened the Mauryan Empire largely without the use of violence.

**How did Ashoka maintain and expand his empire after turning away from military conquest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: After witnessing the destruction of war, Ashoka embraced Buddhism and promoted peace to unify his empire.
Ashoka relied on a powerful navy to block his enemies without ever needing to fight on land.
He placed his empire under the control of local rulers and avoided involvement in political affairs.
Ashoka withdrew completely from governing and left expansion to Buddhist monks and priests.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Legalism shaped Qin Dynasty rule under Lord Shang. Which idea was central to this philosophy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Strict laws and harsh punishments were necessary to maintain order and control.
Rulers should lead by example through kindness and moral virtue alone.
Power should be shared equally between nobles and common citizens.
Education and debate among scholars should guide political decisions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What kinds of obstacles did merchants face during the Zhou and Qin Dynasties?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Confucian and Legalist beliefs viewed merchants as socially disruptive and morally questionable.
Trade routes were so dangerous that merchants needed military training to survive.
The use of coins backfired and caused chaos due to forgery and unstable currency.
Merchants were respected for spreading new ideas, which the government encouraged.
